transcript:
Interviewer: Thanks for joining, Maya. Can you walk me through how your team currently handles reporting?

Maya: Sure. So right now we're exporting data from four different tools — our CRM, our project management tool, our billing system, and our support platform — and then someone on my team manually compiles it into a spreadsheet every Monday morning. It takes about 3–4 hours every week. And honestly, by the time the report is done, some of the data is already outdated.

Interviewer: Is that a problem?

Maya: It's a massive problem. We're making decisions in Thursday leadership meetings based on Monday data. That's almost a week old. And the worst part is when the numbers don't match — like when the CRM shows 45 new accounts but billing only shows 42 — and nobody knows which one is right. So we spend another hour in the meeting trying to reconcile the numbers instead of making decisions.

Interviewer: How do you currently deal with that?

Maya: We've tried a few things. We used Zapier for a while to connect some of the tools, but it kept breaking whenever any of the tools updated their API. We also tried a BI tool — I won't name it — but it required a dedicated data engineer to set it up and maintain it, and we're a team of 12, so that's just not realistic. Right now we're back to the spreadsheet.

Interviewer: What would the ideal solution look like for you?

Maya: Honestly? I just want one dashboard that updates automatically and shows me the 5–6 numbers I actually care about. Revenue, active accounts, open tickets, sprint completion, cash runway. I don't need 50 charts. I need the 6 things that tell me if we're on track. And I want to be able to share it with my board without any manual work.

Interviewer: Any concerns about adopting a new tool?

Maya: Yes, a few. First, our IT team has strict security requirements — any data integration needs to go through a security review. Second, the last two tools we tried required 3-month implementation projects, and they both failed. So I'm very skeptical of anything that promises it'll be up in a week. Third, cost — we're watching every expense right now, and our board will ask why we're paying for another tool.

Interviewer: Is there anything else you'd want me to know?

Maya: Just that the pain is real. My best analyst is spending 4 hours every Monday doing work that a computer should do. That's time she's not spending on actual analysis. If you can solve that, I'll be your biggest advocate.
